audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/osu-lazer-tachyon-bin.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-xw_ypkkg...

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard sources, including an AppImage from the official ppy/osu GitHub repository and auxiliary files from trusted origins (e.g. raw.githubusercontent.com). The `source` array uses direct HTTPS URLs pointing to specific tagged releases, which is a common and safe practice in AUR packages. All checksums are provided and match the expected format.

There is no command substitution, dynamic fetching in top-level scope, or execution outside of the `package()` function—none of which would be triggered by `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare --printsrcinfo`. The `pkgver()` function is absent, so no runtime version calculation occurs during sourcing. No dangerous code (e.g. `curl|bash`, `eval`, or data exfiltration) is present in the global scope.

The package follows standard AUR packaging patterns. While `fuse2` and AppImage usage may raise sandboxing considerations, those are related to runtime behavior and upstream choices, not supply-chain risks during source download and parsing.
</details>
<summary>
Standard sources, no malicious code in parsing phase
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Standard sources, no malicious code in parsing phase[/]

makepkg --printsrcinfo successful

[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD, osu-lazer...
LLM auditresponse for osu-lazer:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script is a simple launcher for the osu! lazer game packaged as an AppImage. It sets three environment variables—`APPIMAGELAUNCHER_DISABLE=1` to prevent AppImageLauncher from intercepting the AppImage, and `OSU_EXTERNAL_UPDATE_PROVIDER=1` and `OSU_EXTERNAL_UPDATE_STREAM=tachyon` to configure the application’s update behavior—before executing the AppImage with all passed arguments.

The script performs no network operations, file downloads, obfuscated code execution, or system modifications. It directly invokes a local binary located in `/opt/osu-lazer/`, which is a standard location for manually installed software. The use of environment variables to control update behavior is consistent with the application’s known support for external update providers and does not introduce security risks.

All operations are transparent and limited to launching a single executable with predictable arguments. There are no signs of malicious behavior such as data exfiltration, remote code execution, or unauthorized system access.
</details>
<summary>
Simple AppImage launcher with no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed osu-lazer. Status: SAFE -- Simple AppImage launcher with no malicious behavior
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a package for the Tachyon pre-release channel of osu-lazer, sourced from the official GitHub repository of the upstream project (ppy/osu). The source URL points to a specific release tag (`$_pkgtag`) on `github.com/ppy/osu`, which is the legitimate upstream source. The AppImage binary is fetched directly from this trusted location and installed into the system.

All other included files (icons, desktop entries, license) are also pulled from the official `ppy/osu-resources` repository or are local assets, consistent with standard packaging practices. Checksums are provided for all sources and do not use 'SKIP'. The build script does not execute arbitrary code, perform network operations at runtime, or modify system state beyond installing the application into standard directories (`/opt`, `/usr/bin`, `/usr/share`). No obfuscated code, dangerous commands (e.g. eval, base64 decoding), or remote execution patterns are present.

The package depends only on standard system libraries and provides appropriate metadata. While it disables stripping and debug info (`!strip !debug`), this is acceptable for a `-bin` package distributing a prebuilt AppImage. The use of `fuse2` to run the AppImage is expected and not inherently unsafe.
</details>
<summary>
Standard AUR packaging; all sources trustworthy
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...

LLM auditresponse for osu-lazer.desktop:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a standard desktop entry file (`osu-lazer.desktop`) used to integrate the application with the desktop environment. It declares metadata such as the application name, icon, executable path, MIME type associations, and category. The `Exec` field points to `/usr/bin/osu-lazer`, a standard system location for binaries, which is appropriate for a packaged application. The `Icon` and `Name` fields are also correctly specified and do not contain suspicious elements.

There are no executable commands, network operations, file manipulations, or any form of code in this file. The MIME types declared are specific to the osu! game (beatmaps, skins, replays) and are reasonable for the application's functionality. No obfuscation, unexpected behaviors, or malicious patterns are present. This file serves only to register the application in the desktop menu and handle associated file types, consistent with freedesktop.org standards.

All content in the file aligns with standard packaging practices for desktop integration on Linux systems. There is no evidence of data exfiltration, remote code execution, or supply-chain compromise.
</details>
<summary>
Standard desktop entry; no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/3] Reviewing osu-lazer-uri-handler.desktop, LICENCE.md...
+ Reviewed osu-lazer.desktop. Status: SAFE -- Standard desktop entry; no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for osu-lazer-uri-handler.desktop:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `osu-lazer-uri-handler.desktop` is a standard desktop entry file used to register a URI handler for the `osu:` protocol in desktop environments that support the xdg-specifications. It declares the MIME type `x-scheme-handler/osu`, sets the executable to `/usr/bin/osu-lazer` with the URI argument `%u`, and points to a legitimate application icon. All fields are consistent with standard desktop file practices for application integration.

There are no signs of malicious behavior, such as suspicious command execution, embedded scripts, or network operations. The `Exec` line calls a fixed binary with a standard argument placeholder, which is normal for URI handlers. The file does not perform any dynamic code loading, obfuscation, or file system traversal beyond what is expected for application launching. The use of `NoDisplay=true` is typical for auxiliary handlers that should not appear in application menus directly.

This file serves a clear functional purpose — enabling deep links to osu!lazer — and does not introduce any supply-chain risks or security vulnerabilities. No data exfiltration, privilege escalation, or unauthorized system access mechanisms are present.
</details>
<summary>
Standard desktop entry file, no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/3] Reviewing LICENCE.md...
+ Reviewed osu-lazer-uri-handler.desktop. Status: SAFE -- Standard desktop entry file, no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for LICENCE.md:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is the text of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International Public License (CC BY-NC 4.0). It is a standard legal document describing the terms under which creative works may be shared and adapted. The content is purely textual and contains no executable code, scripts, or commands. There are no instances of `curl`, `wget`, `eval`, `base64`, `exec`, or any other potentially dangerous constructs. The file does not perform any system operations, network requests, or file manipulations. It is intended solely for human or legal review and is commonly included in software packages to describe licensing terms.

Including this license file in a package like `osu-lazer-tachyon-bin` is standard practice when the software or its assets are distributed under this license. The presence of this file poses no security risk, as it cannot be executed and does not contain any hidden or obfuscated payloads. It aligns with expected packaging behavior and serves a legitimate purpose.

The file is safe and consistent with standard open-source licensing practices.
</details>
<summary>
Standard license file, no executable code or threats.
</summary>
</security_assessment>
